revision.text: #redirect[[en:Kuroyu]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]
'''黒湯(くろゆ)'''とは、主に東京都[[大田区]]および周辺地域で多く見られる黒褐色の[[温泉]]の俗称。植物性の[[有機物]]が多く含まれることにより黒褐色となっている。温泉と言っても[[旅館]]形式ではなく、昔ながらの庶民的な[[銭湯]]として営業している。

'''黒湯''' * [[秋田県]][[仙北市]]の[[黒湯温泉]]のこと。リンク先参照。 * 黒色の温泉のこと。本項で述べる。
---- '''黒湯'''(くろゆ)とは、主に[[風呂|湯船]]における湯の色が黒色、黒褐色をした[[源泉]]のことを指す。東京都[[大田区]]および周辺地域で多く見られることから、この地域の温泉の俗称ともなっている。
黒色を呈する源泉のタイプは2種類存在する。大半が、[[モール泉]]同様植物性の[[有機物]]が多く含まれることによる黒湯である。他方は黒色の[[湯の花]]による黒湯である。[[塩原温泉郷]]の塩原元湯温泉など、ごく一部にのみ存在する。
[[Category:温泉|くろゆ]]

'''黒湯''' * [[秋田県]][[仙北市]]の[[黒湯温泉]]のこと。リンク先参照。 * 黒色の[[温泉]]のこと。本項で述べる。
---- '''黒湯'''(くろゆ)とは、主に[[風呂|湯船]]における湯の色が黒色、黒褐色をした[[源泉]]のことを指す。東京都[[大田区]]および周辺地域で多く見られることから、この地域の温泉の俗称ともなっている。
黒色を呈する源泉のタイプは2種類存在する。大半が、[[モール泉]]同様植物性の[[有機物]]が多く含まれることによる黒湯である。他方は黒色の[[湯の花]]による黒湯である。[[塩原温泉郷]]の塩原元湯温泉など、ごく一部にのみ存在する。
[[Category:温泉|くろゆ]]

'''黒湯''' * [[秋田県]][[仙北市]]の[[黒湯温泉]]のこと。リンク先参照。 * 黒色の[[温泉]]のこと。本項で述べる。
---- '''黒湯'''(くろゆ)とは、主に[[風呂|湯船]]における湯の色が黒色、黒褐色をした[[源泉]]のことを指す。東京都[[大田区]]および周辺地域で多く見られることから、この地域の温泉の俗称ともなっている。
黒色を呈する源泉のタイプは2種類存在する。大半が、[[モール泉]]同様植物性の[[有機物]]が多く含まれることによる黒湯である。他方は黒色の[[湯の花]]による黒湯である。[[塩原温泉郷]]の[[塩原元湯温泉]]など、ごく一部にのみ存在する。
[[Category:温泉|くろゆ]]

{{otheruses|黒色の[[温泉]]|[[秋田県]][[仙北市]]にある温泉|黒湯温泉}}
'''黒湯'''(くろゆ)とは、主に[[風呂|湯船]]における湯の色が黒色、黒褐色をした[[源泉]]のことを指す。東京都[[大田区]]および周辺地域で多く見られることから、この地域の温泉の俗称ともなっている。
黒色を呈する源泉のタイプは2種類存在する。大半が、[[モール泉]]同様植物性の[[有機物]]が多く含まれることによる黒湯である。他方は黒色の[[湯の花]]による黒湯である。[[塩原温泉郷]]の[[塩原元湯温泉]]など、ごく一部にのみ存在する。
[[Category:温泉|くろゆ]]

{{otheruses|黒色の[[温泉]]|[[秋田県]][[仙北市]]にある温泉|黒湯温泉}}
'''黒湯'''（くろゆ）とは、主に[[風呂|湯船]]における湯の色が[[黒|黒色]]、[[茶色|黒褐色]]をした[[源泉]]または[[温泉]]を指す。
== 概要 == 色の黒さに特色をもつ[[鉱泉]]または温泉である。黒さとともに、フミン酸や塩分を含んでいたり、粘度が比較的高い特色をもつ場合が多い。
=== 黒湯の成分 === 黒湯における[[泉質]]の分類としては[[炭酸水素塩泉]]が多くを占め、続いて[[塩化物泉]]、その他の温泉からなる<ref>[http://www.soshigaya-onsen21.com/quality/quality_03.html 泉質：黒湯の特殊性]（そしがや温泉21）</ref>。
黒色を呈する要素としては、おおむね3種類存在する。 ;植物性の有機物によるもの :大半が[[モール泉]]同様、植物性の[[有機物]]（[[フミン酸]]など）が多く含まれることによる黒湯である（分布はモール泉の項を参照）。
;ヨウ素によるもの :[[千葉県]]や[[東京都]]の都心から東部にかけては[[南関東ガス田]]と呼ばれるガス田地帯であり、黒湯はこれらの灌水（かんすい）として湧出する。南関東ガス田の特色として、[[天然ガス]]だけでなく塩分や[[ヨウ素]]を豊富に含んでおり、その色が黒さに反映されている。
;硫化物によるもの :[[硫化物]]によって黒色となった[[湯の花]]による黒湯である。[[塩原温泉郷]]の[[塩原元湯温泉]]など、ごく一部にのみ存在する。
=== 東京都大田区の黒湯温泉 === 東京都[[大田区]]一帯の[[大田区の黒湯温泉|黒湯温泉]]がよく知られており、この地域の黒湯を使った[[銭湯]]や[[日帰り入浴施設]]を含む温泉の俗称ともなっていたが<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80563/ 黒さでは日本有数、東京蒲田周辺の黒湯（ALL About 2008年6月22日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>、周辺地域の温泉の掘削数が増えるにつれて、同種の温泉が[[板橋区]]や[[台東区]]、[[墨田区]]、[[江戸川区]]、[[江東区]]、[[新宿区]]、[[世田谷区]]、[[品川区]]、[[港区 (東京都)|港区]]など東京23区の過半数を超える区や<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80528/ 東京都内に日本一の濃い黒湯があった [温泉] All About 2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref><ref>[http://shibaku.s74.xrea.com/tokyo.html 東京の黒湯情報室【東京の温泉銭湯・黒湯銭湯】2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref>、[[多摩地区]]、[[横浜市]]、[[相模原市]]などでも見られるようになり、名称がそのまま転用されることがある<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80518/2/ 神奈川県の日帰り温泉4ヶ所（前編）（ALL About 2007年3月5日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>。
== 出典 == <references/>
{{温泉}} {{onsen-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:くろゆ}}
[[Category:温泉]] [[Category:大田区]]

{{otheruses|黒色の[[温泉]]|[[秋田県]][[仙北市]]にある温泉|黒湯温泉}}
'''黒湯'''（くろゆ）とは、主に[[風呂|湯船]]における湯の色が[[黒|黒色]]、[[茶色|黒褐色]]をした[[源泉]]または[[温泉]]を指す。
== 概要 == 色の黒さに特色をもつ[[鉱泉]]または温泉である。黒さとともに、フミン酸や塩分を含んでいたり、粘度が比較的高い特色をもつ場合が多い。
=== 黒湯の成分 === 黒湯における[[泉質]]の分類としては[[炭酸水素塩泉]]が多くを占め、続いて[[塩化物泉]]、その他の温泉からなる<ref>[http://www.soshigaya-onsen21.com/quality/quality_03.html 泉質：黒湯の特殊性]（そしがや温泉21）</ref>。
黒色を呈する要素としては、おおむね2種類存在する。 ;植物性の有機物によるもの :大半が[[モール泉]]同様、植物性の[[有機物]]（[[フミン酸]]など）が多く含まれることによる黒湯である（分布はモール泉の項を参照）。[[千葉県]]や[[東京都]]の都心から東部にかけては[[南関東ガス田]]と呼ばれる水溶性のガス田地帯であり、これらのガス掘削目的から湧出されて発見された黒湯もある。南関東ガス田の特色として、天然ガス以外にヨウ素を豊富に含んでおり、湯の色を一層深くしている。
;硫化物によるもの :[[硫化物]]によって黒色となった[[湯の花]]による黒湯である。[[塩原温泉郷]]の[[塩原元湯温泉]]など、ごく一部にのみ存在する。
=== 東京都大田区の黒湯温泉 === 東京都[[大田区]]一帯の[[大田区の黒湯温泉|黒湯温泉]]がよく知られており、この地域の黒湯を使った[[銭湯]]や[[日帰り入浴施設]]を含む温泉の俗称ともなっていたが<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80563/ 黒さでは日本有数、東京蒲田周辺の黒湯（ALL About 2008年6月22日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>、周辺地域の温泉の掘削数が増えるにつれて、同種の温泉が[[板橋区]]や[[台東区]]、[[墨田区]]、[[江戸川区]]、[[江東区]]、[[新宿区]]、[[世田谷区]]、[[品川区]]、[[港区 (東京都)|港区]]など東京23区の過半数を超える区や<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80528/ 東京都内に日本一の濃い黒湯があった [温泉] All About 2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref><ref>[http://shibaku.s74.xrea.com/tokyo.html 東京の黒湯情報室【東京の温泉銭湯・黒湯銭湯】2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref>、[[多摩地区]]、[[横浜市]]、[[相模原市]]などでも見られるようになり、名称がそのまま転用されることがある<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80518/2/ 神奈川県の日帰り温泉4ヶ所（前編）（ALL About 2007年3月5日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>。
== 出典 == <references/>

=== 東京都大田区の黒湯温泉 === 東京都[[大田区]]一帯の[[大田区の黒湯温泉|黒湯温泉]]がよく知られており、この地域の黒湯を使った[[銭湯]]や[[日帰り入浴施設]]を含む温泉の俗称ともなっていたが<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80563/ 黒さでは日本有数、東京蒲田周辺の黒湯（ALL About 2008年6月22日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>、周辺地域の温泉の掘削数が増えるにつれて、同種の温泉が[[板橋区]]や[[台東区]]、[[墨田区]]、[[江戸川区]]、[[江東区]]、[[新宿区]]、[[世田谷区]]、[[品川区]]、[[港区 (東京都)|港区]]など東京23区の過半数を超える区や<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80528/ 東京都内に日本一の濃い黒湯があった [温泉] All About 2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref><ref>[http://shibaku.s74.xrea.com/tokyo.html 東京の黒湯情報室【東京の温泉銭湯・黒湯銭湯】2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=要検証}}、[[多摩地区]]、[[横浜市]]、[[相模原市]]などでも見られるようになり、名称がそのまま転用されることがある<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80518/2/ 神奈川県の日帰り温泉4ヶ所（前編）（ALL About 2007年3月5日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>。

{{otheruses|黒色の[[温泉]]|[[秋田県]][[仙北市]]にある温泉|黒湯温泉}}
'''黒湯'''（くろゆ）とは、主に[[風呂|湯船]]における湯の色が[[黒|黒色]]、[[茶色|黒褐色]]をした[[源泉]]または[[温泉]]を指す。
== 概要 == 色の黒さに特色をもつ[[鉱泉]]または温泉である。黒さとともに、フミン酸や塩分を含んでいたり、粘度が比較的高い特色をもつ場合が多い。
=== 黒湯の成分 === 黒湯における[[泉質]]の分類としては[[炭酸水素塩泉]]が多くを占め、続いて[[塩化物泉]]、その他の温泉からなる<ref>[http://www.soshigaya-onsen21.com/quality/quality_03.html 泉質：黒湯の特殊性]（そしがや温泉21）</ref>。
黒色を呈する要素としては、おおむね2種類存在する。 ;植物性の有機物によるもの :大半が[[モール泉]]同様、植物性の[[有機物]]（[[フミン酸]]など）が多く含まれることによる黒湯である（分布はモール泉の項を参照）。[[千葉県]]や[[東京都]]の都心から東部にかけては[[南関東ガス田]]と呼ばれる水溶性のガス田地帯であり、これらのガス掘削目的から湧出されて発見された黒湯もある。南関東ガス田の特色として、天然ガス以外に[[ヨウ素]]を豊富に含んでおり、湯の色を一層深くしている。
;硫化物によるもの :[[硫化物]]によって黒色となった[[湯の花]]による黒湯である。[[塩原温泉郷]]の[[塩原元湯温泉]]など、ごく一部にのみ存在する。
=== 東京都大田区の黒湯温泉 === 東京都[[大田区]]一帯の[[大田区の黒湯温泉|黒湯温泉]]がよく知られており、この地域の黒湯を使った[[銭湯]]や[[日帰り入浴施設]]を含む温泉の俗称ともなっていたが<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80563/ 黒さでは日本有数、東京蒲田周辺の黒湯（ALL About 2008年6月22日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>、周辺地域の温泉の掘削数が増えるにつれて、同種の温泉が[[板橋区]]や[[台東区]]、[[墨田区]]、[[江戸川区]]、[[江東区]]、[[新宿区]]、[[世田谷区]]、[[品川区]]、[[港区 (東京都)|港区]]など東京23区の過半数を超える区や<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80528/ 東京都内に日本一の濃い黒湯があった [温泉] All About 2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref><ref>[http://shibaku.s74.xrea.com/tokyo.html 東京の黒湯情報室【東京の温泉銭湯・黒湯銭湯】2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=要検証（記事が古いため）}}、[[多摩地区]]、[[横浜市]]、[[相模原市]]などでも見られるようになり、名称がそのまま転用されることがある<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80518/2/ 神奈川県の日帰り温泉4ヶ所（前編）（ALL About 2007年3月5日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>。

黒色を呈する要素としては、おおむね2種類存在する。 ;植物性の有機物によるもの :大半が[[モール泉]]同様、植物性の[[有機物]]（[[フミン酸]]など）が多く含まれることによる黒湯である（分布はモール泉の項を参照）。[[千葉県]]や[[東京都]]の都心から東部にかけては[[南関東ガス田]]と呼ばれる水溶性のガス田地帯であり、これらのガス掘削目的から湧出されて発見された黒湯もある。南関東ガス田の特色として、天然ガス以外に[[ヨウ素]]を豊富に含んでおり、湯の色を一層深くしている。
;硫化物によるもの :[[硫化物]]によって黒色となった[[湯の花]]による黒湯である。[[塩原温泉郷]]の[[塩原元湯温泉]]など、ごく一部にのみ存在する。
=== 東京都大田区の黒湯温泉 === 東京都[[大田区]]一帯の[[大田区の黒湯温泉|黒湯温泉]]がよく知られており、この地域の黒湯を使った[[銭湯]]や[[日帰り入浴施設]]を含む温泉の俗称ともなっていたが<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80563/ 黒さでは日本有数、東京蒲田周辺の黒湯（ALL About 2008年6月22日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=出典の正確性に欠いています。}}、周辺地域の温泉の掘削数が増えるにつれて、同種の温泉が[[板橋区]]や[[台東区]]、[[墨田区]]、[[江戸川区]]、[[江東区]]、[[新宿区]]、[[世田谷区]]、[[品川区]]、[[港区 (東京都)|港区]]など東京23区の過半数を超える区や<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80528/ 東京都内に日本一の濃い黒湯があった [温泉] All About 2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=出典の正確性に欠いています。}}<ref>[http://shibaku.s74.xrea.com/tokyo.html 東京の黒湯情報室【東京の温泉銭湯・黒湯銭湯】2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=現在のの情報に反映されていません。}}、[[多摩地区]]、[[横浜市]]、[[相模原市]]などでも見られるようになり、名称がそのまま転用されることがある<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80518/2/ 神奈川県の日帰り温泉4ヶ所（前編）（ALL About 2007年3月5日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>。
== 出典 == <references/>
{{温泉}} {{onsen-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:くろゆ}}
[[Category:温泉]] [[Category:大田区]]

{{otheruses|黒色の[[温泉]]|[[秋田県]][[仙北市]]にある温泉|黒湯温泉}} {{言葉を濁さない|date=2013年07月}}
'''黒湯'''（くろゆ）とは、主に[[風呂|湯船]]における湯の色が[[黒|黒色]]、[[茶色|黒褐色]]をした[[源泉]]または[[温泉]]を指す。
== 概要 == 色の黒さに特色をもつ[[鉱泉]]または温泉である。黒さとともに、フミン酸や塩分を含んでいたり、粘度が比較的高い特色をもつ場合が多い。
=== 黒湯の成分 === 黒湯における[[泉質]]の分類としては[[炭酸水素塩泉]]が多くを占め、続いて[[塩化物泉]]、その他の温泉からなる<ref>[http://www.soshigaya-onsen21.com/quality/quality_03.html 泉質：黒湯の特殊性]（そしがや温泉21）</ref>。
黒色を呈する要素としては、おおむね2種類存在する。 ;植物性の有機物によるもの :大半が[[モール泉]]同様、植物性の[[有機物]]（[[フミン酸]]など）が多く含まれることによる黒湯である（分布はモール泉の項を参照）。[[千葉県]]や[[東京都]]の都心から東部にかけては[[南関東ガス田]]と呼ばれる水溶性のガス田地帯であり、これらのガス掘削目的から湧出されて発見された黒湯もある。南関東ガス田の特色として、天然ガス以外に[[ヨウ素]]を豊富に含んでおり、湯の色を一層深くしている。
;硫化物によるもの :[[硫化物]]によって黒色となった[[湯の花]]による黒湯である。[[塩原温泉郷]]の[[塩原元湯温泉]]など、ごく一部にのみ存在する。
=== 東京都大田区の黒湯温泉 === 東京都[[大田区]]一帯の[[大田区の黒湯温泉|黒湯温泉]]がよく知られており、この地域の黒湯を使った[[銭湯]]や[[日帰り入浴施設]]を含む温泉の俗称ともなっていたが<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80563/ 黒さでは日本有数、東京蒲田周辺の黒湯（ALL About 2008年6月22日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=出典の正確性に欠いています。}}、周辺地域の温泉の掘削数が増えるにつれて、同種の温泉が[[板橋区]]や[[台東区]]、[[墨田区]]、[[江戸川区]]、[[江東区]]、[[新宿区]]、[[世田谷区]]、[[品川区]]、[[港区 (東京都)|港区]]など東京23区の過半数を超える区や<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80528/ 東京都内に日本一の濃い黒湯があった [温泉] All About 2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=出典の正確性に欠いています}}<ref>[http://shibaku.s74.xrea.com/tokyo.html 東京の黒湯情報室【東京の温泉銭湯・黒湯銭湯】2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=現在のの情報に反映されていません}}、[[多摩地区]]、[[横浜市]]、[[相模原市]]などでも見られるようになり、名称がそのまま転用されることがある<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80518/2/ 神奈川県の日帰り温泉4ヶ所（前編）（ALL About 2007年3月5日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=出典の正確さに欠いています}}。
== 出典 == <references/>
{{温泉}} {{onsen-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:くろゆ}}
[[Category:温泉]] [[Category:大田区]]

{{otheruses|黒色の[[温泉]]|[[秋田県]][[仙北市]]にある温泉|黒湯温泉}} {{言葉を濁さない|date=2013年07月}}
'''黒湯'''（くろゆ）とは、主に[[風呂|湯船]]における湯の色が[[黒|黒色]]、[[茶色|黒褐色]]をした[[源泉]]または[[温泉]]を指す。
== 概要 == 色の黒さに特色をもつ[[鉱泉]]または温泉である。黒さとともに、フミン酸や塩分を含んでいたり、粘度が比較的高い特色をもつ場合が多い。
=== 黒湯の成分 === 黒湯における[[泉質]]の分類としては[[炭酸水素塩泉]]が多くを占め、続いて[[塩化物泉]]、その他の温泉からなる<ref>[http://www.soshigaya-onsen21.com/quality/quality_03.html 泉質：黒湯の特殊性]（そしがや温泉21）</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-05 |title=正確性を欠いた誇大広告になっています。Wikipediaは広告の場ではありません}}。
黒色を呈する要素としては、おおむね2種類存在する。 ;植物性の有機物によるもの :大半が[[モール泉]]同様、植物性の[[有機物]]（[[フミン酸]]など）が多く含まれることによる黒湯である（分布はモール泉の項を参照）。[[千葉県]]や[[東京都]]の都心から東部にかけては[[南関東ガス田]]と呼ばれる水溶性のガス田地帯であり、これらのガス掘削目的から湧出されて発見された黒湯もある。南関東ガス田の特色として、天然ガス以外に[[ヨウ素]]を豊富に含んでおり、湯の色を一層深くしている。

=== 東京都大田区の黒湯温泉 === 東京都[[大田区]]一帯の[[大田区の黒湯温泉|黒湯温泉]]がよく知られており、この地域の黒湯を使った[[銭湯]]や[[日帰り入浴施設]]を含む温泉の俗称ともなっていたが<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80563/ 黒さでは日本有数、東京蒲田周辺の黒湯（ALL About 2008年6月22日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=出典の正確性に欠いています。}}、周辺地域の温泉の掘削数が増えるにつれて、同種の温泉が[[板橋区]]や[[台東区]]、[[墨田区]]、[[江戸川区]]、[[江東区]]、[[新宿区]]、[[世田谷区]]、[[品川区]]、[[港区 (東京都)|港区]]など東京23区の過半数を超える区や<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80528/ 東京都内に日本一の濃い黒湯があった [温泉] All About 2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=出典の正確性に欠いています}}<ref>[http://shibaku.s74.xrea.com/tokyo.html 東京の黒湯情報室【東京の温泉銭湯・黒湯銭湯】2012年7月10日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=現在のの情報に反映されていません}}、[[多摩地区]]、[[横浜市]]、[[相模原市]]などでも見られるようになり、名称がそのまま転用されることがある<ref>[http://allabout.co.jp/gm/gc/80518/2/ 神奈川県の日帰り温泉4ヶ所（前編）（ALL About 2007年3月5日）2011年10月15日閲覧]</ref>{{出典無効|date=2013-07-06 |title=出典の正確さに欠いています}}。

/* 概要 */ 問題指摘用タグがたくさん貼られているが、参照の外部リンクのサイトを見てみたら、たしかに、 「信頼できる情報源」ではなさそう

revision.text:
{{otheruses|[[温泉]]の一種|[[秋田県]][[仙北市]]にある温泉|黒湯温泉}} '''黒湯'''（くろゆ）とは、[[フミン酸]]や[[フルボ酸]]といった透明で褐色の腐植物質を含む温泉<ref name="inoue" />。このほか[[硫化鉄]]など別の理由で湯の色が[[黒|黒色]]を呈していて「黒湯」あるいは「墨湯」と呼ばれているものもある<ref name="gifu" />。
== 概要 == 溶存有機物（DOM）であるフミン酸やフルボ酸で構成される腐植物質によって褐色に着色したいわゆる「褐色温泉」である<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=内野 栄治 |author3= 青柳 直樹 |title=北海道内で湧出する褐色温泉に含まれる溶存有機物質及び腐植物質中の炭素、窒素及びリン含量 |journal=陸水学雑誌 |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=日本陸水學會 |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>。褐色温泉には「黒湯」あるいは「モール系温泉」（[[モール泉]]）と呼ばれるものがあり、地域によって、[[北海道]]などでは「モール系温泉」、[[関東地方]]などでは「黒湯」と呼ばれている<ref name="takano2018" />。
個々の泉質とは別に、褐色温泉であることによる適応症が正式に定義されているわけではないが、一般には皮膚をなめらかにするといった効果がうたわれる<ref name="takano2018" />。
[[関東地方|関東]]では[[東京都]][[大田区の黒湯温泉]]などが知られる<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=井上 源喜 |author2=清水 理紗子 |author3= 名倉 美佳 |title=東京都大田区地域における黒湯の地球化学的特徴 |journal=日本地球化学会年会要旨集 |volume=66 |issue= |publisher=一般社団法人日本地球化学会 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>。
== 溶存有機物以外の黒湯 == 「黒湯」と称される温泉には、植物が微生物によって分解された腐植質を含む地層に由来するものとは別に、硫化水素型の硫黄泉などで[[硫化水素]]が[[鉄]]が化合して黒色の硫化鉄を析出して懸濁しているものなどもある<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=第13話 様々な温泉の色 |publisher=岐阜県温泉協会 |date= |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>。硫化鉄の析出による黒湯がみられる例として[[塩原元湯温泉]]があり「墨湯」ともいう<ref name="gifu" />。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <!-- === 注釈 === <references group="注"/> === 出典 === --> {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[にごり湯]]
{{温泉}} {{デフォルトソート:くろゆ}} [[Category:温泉]]

{{otheruses|[[温泉]]の一種|[[秋田県]][[仙北市]]にある温泉|黒湯温泉}} '''黒湯'''（くろゆ）とは、[[フミン酸]]や[[フルボ酸]]といった透明で褐色の腐植物質を含む温泉<ref name="inoue" />。このほか[[硫化鉄]]など別の理由で湯の色が[[黒|黒色]]を呈していて「黒湯」あるいは「墨湯」と呼ばれているものもある<ref name="gifu" />。
== 概要 == 溶存有機物（DOM）であるフミン酸やフルボ酸で構成される腐植物質によって褐色に着色したいわゆる「褐色温泉」である<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=内野 栄治 |author3= 青柳 直樹 |title=北海道内で湧出する褐色温泉に含まれる溶存有機物質及び腐植物質中の炭素、窒素及びリン含量 |journal=陸水学雑誌 |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=日本陸水學會 |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>。褐色温泉には「黒湯」あるいは「モール系温泉」（[[モール泉]]）と呼ばれるものがあり、地域によって、[[北海道]]などでは「モール系温泉」、[[関東地方]]などでは「黒湯」と呼ばれている<ref name="takano2018" />。ただ名称だけでなく成分についても、神奈川県横浜・川崎地域の黒湯と北海道のモール系温泉を比較すると、黒湯のほうが[[C/N比]]が低く[[C/P比]]が高いという報告がある<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=井上 源喜 |author3= 内野 栄治 |title=炭素/窒素比および炭素/リン比からみた神奈川県横浜・川崎地域に湧出する黒湯に含まれる腐植物質の特性 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=70 |issue=3 |publisher= |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>。
個々の泉質とは別に、褐色温泉であることによる適応症が正式に定義されているわけではないが、一般には皮膚をなめらかにするといった効果がうたわれる<ref name="takano2018" />。
[[関東地方|関東]]では[[東京都]][[大田区の黒湯温泉]]などが知られる<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=井上 源喜 |author2=清水 理紗子 |author3= 名倉 美佳 |title=東京都大田区地域における黒湯の地球化学的特徴 |journal=日本地球化学会年会要旨集 |volume=66 |issue= |publisher=一般社団法人日本地球化学会 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>。
== 溶存有機物以外の黒湯 == 「黒湯」と称される温泉には、植物が微生物によって分解された腐植質を含む地層に由来するものとは別に、硫化水素型の硫黄泉などで[[硫化水素]]が[[鉄]]が化合して黒色の硫化鉄を析出して懸濁しているものなどもある<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=第13話 様々な温泉の色 |publisher=岐阜県温泉協会 |date= |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>。硫化鉄の析出による黒湯がみられる例として[[塩原元湯温泉]]があり「墨湯」ともいう<ref name="gifu" />。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <!-- === 注釈 === <references group="注"/> === 出典 === --> {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[にごり湯]]
{{温泉}} {{デフォルトソート:くろゆ}} [[Category:温泉]]

{{otheruses|[[温泉]]の一種|[[秋田県]][[仙北市]]にある温泉|黒湯温泉}} '''黒湯'''（くろゆ）とは、[[フミン酸]]や[[フルボ酸]]といった透明で褐色の腐植物質を含む温泉<ref name="inoue" />。このほか[[硫化鉄]]など別の理由で湯の色が[[黒|黒色]]を呈していて「黒湯」あるいは「墨湯」と呼ばれているものもある<ref name="gifu" />。
== 概要 == 溶存有機物（DOM）であるフミン酸やフルボ酸で構成される腐植物質によって褐色に着色したいわゆる「褐色温泉」である<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=内野 栄治 |author3= 青柳 直樹 |title=北海道内で湧出する褐色温泉に含まれる溶存有機物質及び腐植物質中の炭素、窒素及びリン含量 |journal=陸水学雑誌 |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=日本陸水學會 |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>。褐色温泉には「黒湯」あるいは「モール系温泉」（[[モール泉]]）と呼ばれるものがあり、地域によって、[[北海道]]などでは「モール系温泉」、[[関東地方]]などでは「黒湯」と呼ばれている<ref name="takano2018" />。ただ名称だけでなく成分についても、神奈川県横浜・川崎地域の黒湯と北海道のモール系温泉を比較すると、黒湯のほうが[[C/N比]]が低く[[C/P比]]が高いという報告がある<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=井上 源喜 |author3= 内野 栄治 |title=炭素/窒素比および炭素/リン比からみた神奈川県横浜・川崎地域に湧出する黒湯に含まれる腐植物質の特性 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=70 |issue=3 |publisher= |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>。
個々の泉質とは別に、褐色温泉であることによる適応症が正式に定義されているわけではないが、一般には皮膚をなめらかにするといった効果がうたわれる<ref name="takano2018" />。
[[関東地方|関東]]では[[東京都]][[大田区の黒湯温泉]]などが知られる<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=井上 源喜 |author2=清水 理紗子 |author3= 名倉 美佳 |title=東京都大田区地域における黒湯の地球化学的特徴 |journal=日本地球化学会年会要旨集 |volume=66 |issue= |publisher=一般社団法人日本地球化学会 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>。
== 溶存有機物以外の黒湯 == 「黒湯」と称される温泉には、植物が微生物によって分解された腐植質を含む地層に由来するものとは別に、硫化水素型の硫黄泉などで[[硫化水素]]が[[鉄]]が化合して黒色の硫化鉄を析出して懸濁しているものなどもある<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=第13話 様々な温泉の色 |publisher=岐阜県温泉協会 |date= |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>。硫化鉄の析出による黒湯がみられる例として[[塩原元湯温泉]]があり「墨湯」ともいう<ref name="gifu" />。 * [[塩原元湯温泉]]<ref name="gifu" /> * [[岩室温泉]]<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=区役所だより にしかん 2023年8月号 |publisher=新潟市西蒲区 |date= |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <!-- === 注釈 === <references group="注"/> === 出典 === --> {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[にごり湯]]
{{温泉}} {{デフォルトソート:くろゆ}} [[Category:温泉]]

revision.comment: Created page with "{{otheruses|{{ill|hot spring|ja|温泉}} type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉}}, a hot spring in {{ill|Semboku, Akita Prefecture|ja|仙北市}}}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />. == Overview..."
revision.text:
{{otheruses|{{ill|hot spring|ja|温泉}} type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉}}, a hot spring in {{ill|Semboku, Akita Prefecture|ja|仙北市}}}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}

{{温泉}} [[Category:Hot springs]] [[ja:黒湯]]

Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:

Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.

Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.

Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.

This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. {{otheruses|{{ill|hot spring|ja|温泉}} type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉}}, a hot spring in {{ill|Semboku, Akita Prefecture|ja|仙北市}}}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Murky Hot Springs|ja|にごり湯}}
{{温泉}} [[Category:Hot springs]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kuroyu-qq]] [[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kuroyu-qq]] [[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kuroyu-qq]] [[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Kuroyu]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Onsen water qualities]] [[Category:taggint in namespace ]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:黒湯]]

{{otheruses|{{ill|hot spring|ja|温泉}} type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉}}, a hot spring in {{ill|Semboku, Akita Prefecture|ja|仙北市}}}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.

Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.

This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. {{温泉}} [[Category:Hot springs]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kuroyu-qq]] [[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kuroyu-qq]] [[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kuroyu-qq]] [[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:Kuroyu]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kuroyu]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Onsen water qualities]] [[Category:taggint in namespace ]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:黒湯]] [[Category:ppp]]

{{otheruses|{{ill|hot spring|ja|温泉}} type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉}}, a hot spring in {{ill|Semboku, Akita Prefecture|ja|仙北市}}}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot spring}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

{{otheruses|{{ill|hot spring|ja|温泉}} type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉}}, a hot spring in {{ill|Semboku, Akita Prefecture|ja|仙北市}}}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot spring}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot spring}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

[[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kuroyu]][[Category:11]] [[ja:黒湯]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] {{Onsen}} [[Category:New]]

{{otheruses|{{ill|hot spring|ja|温泉}} type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉}}, a hot spring in {{ill|Semboku, Akita Prefecture|ja|仙北市}}}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot spring}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

{{otheruses|{{ill|hot spring|ja|温泉}} type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉}}, a hot spring in {{ill|Semboku, Akita Prefecture|ja|仙北市}}}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot spring}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot spring}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.

There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比|12=draft}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比|12=draft}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.

This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=draft}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=draft}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

{{otheruses|{{ill|hot spring|ja|温泉|12=draft}} type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉|12=draft}}, a hot spring in {{ill|Semboku, Akita Prefecture|ja|仙北市|12=draft}}}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot spring|12=draft}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|ja|北海道|12=draft}}, while in the {{ill|Kanto region|ja|関東地方|12=draft}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|C/N ratio|ja|C/N比|12=draft}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比|12=draft}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉|12=draft}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=draft}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=draft}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Murky Hot Springs|ja|にごり湯|12=draft}}

Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=draft}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=draft}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>
