revision.comment: Bot: single‑shot reformat + ill→|12=draft + draft categories + iws + DEFAULTSORT + move HTML comments + meta‑cat check
59 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:黒湯 up to rev 99626431
revision.text:
{{otheruses|[[温泉]]の一種|[[秋田県]][[仙北市]]にある温泉|黒湯温泉}} '''黒湯'''（くろゆ）とは、[[フミン酸]]や[[フルボ酸]]といった透明で褐色の腐植物質を含む温泉<ref name="inoue" />。このほか[[硫化鉄]]など別の理由で湯の色が[[黒|黒色]]を呈していて「黒湯」あるいは「墨湯」と呼ばれているものもある<ref name="gifu" />。
== 概要 == 溶存有機物（DOM）であるフミン酸やフルボ酸で構成される腐植物質によって褐色に着色したいわゆる「褐色温泉」である<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=内野 栄治 |author3= 青柳 直樹 |title=北海道内で湧出する褐色温泉に含まれる溶存有機物質及び腐植物質中の炭素、窒素及びリン含量 |journal=陸水学雑誌 |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=日本陸水學會 |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>。褐色温泉には「黒湯」あるいは「モール系温泉」（[[モール泉]]）と呼ばれるものがあり、地域によって、[[北海道]]などでは「モール系温泉」、[[関東地方]]などでは「黒湯」と呼ばれている<ref name="takano2018" />。ただ名称だけでなく成分についても、神奈川県横浜・川崎地域の黒湯と北海道のモール系温泉を比較すると、黒湯のほうが[[C/N比]]が低く[[C/P比]]が高いという報告がある<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=井上 源喜 |author3= 内野 栄治 |title=炭素/窒素比および炭素/リン比からみた神奈川県横浜・川崎地域に湧出する黒湯に含まれる腐植物質の特性 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=70 |issue=3 |publisher= |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>。
個々の泉質とは別に、褐色温泉であることによる適応症が正式に定義されているわけではないが、一般には皮膚をなめらかにするといった効果がうたわれる<ref name="takano2018" />。
[[関東地方|関東]]では[[東京都]][[大田区の黒湯温泉]]などが知られる<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=井上 源喜 |author2=清水 理紗子 |author3= 名倉 美佳 |title=東京都大田区地域における黒湯の地球化学的特徴 |journal=日本地球化学会年会要旨集 |volume=66 |issue= |publisher=一般社団法人日本地球化学会 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>。
== 溶存有機物以外の黒湯 == 「黒湯」と称される温泉には、植物が微生物によって分解された腐植質を含む地層に由来するものとは別に、硫化水素型の硫黄泉などで[[硫化水素]]が[[鉄]]が化合して黒色の硫化鉄を析出して懸濁しているものなどもある<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=第13話 様々な温泉の色 |publisher=岐阜県温泉協会 |date= |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>。硫化鉄の析出による黒湯がみられる例として[[塩原元湯温泉]]があり「墨湯」ともいう<ref name="gifu" />。 * [[塩原元湯温泉]]<ref name="gifu" /> * [[岩室温泉]]<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=区役所だより にしかん 2023年8月号 |publisher=新潟市西蒲区 |date= |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <!-- === 注釈 === <references group="注"/> === 出典 === --> {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[にごり湯]]
{{温泉}} {{デフォルトソート:くろゆ}} [[Category:温泉]]

== 概要 == 溶存有機物（DOM）であるフミン酸やフルボ酸で構成される腐植物質によって褐色に着色したいわゆる「褐色温泉」である<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=内野 栄治 |author3= 青柳 直樹 |title=北海道内で湧出する褐色温泉に含まれる溶存有機物質及び腐植物質中の炭素、窒素及びリン含量 |journal=陸水学雑誌 |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=日本陸水學會 |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>。褐色温泉には「黒湯」あるいは「モール系温泉」（[[モール泉]]）と呼ばれるものがあり、地域によって、[[北海道]]などでは「モール系温泉」、[[関東地方]]などでは「黒湯」と呼ばれている<ref name="takano2018" />。ただ名称だけでなく成分についても、神奈川県横浜・川崎地域の黒湯と北海道のモール系温泉を比較すると、黒湯のほうが[[C/N比]]が低く[[C/P比]]が高いという報告がある<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=高野 敬志 |author2=井上 源喜 |author3= 内野 栄治 |title=炭素/窒素比および炭素/リン比からみた神奈川県横浜・川崎地域に湧出する黒湯に含まれる腐植物質の特性 |journal=温泉科学 |volume=70 |issue=3 |publisher= |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>。
個々の泉質とは別に、褐色温泉であることによる適応症が正式に定義されているわけではないが、一般には皮膚をなめらかにするといった効果がうたわれる<ref name="takano2018" />。
[[関東地方|関東]]では[[東京都]][[大田区の黒湯温泉]]などが知られる<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=井上 源喜 |author2=清水 理紗子 |author3= 名倉 美佳 |title=東京都大田区地域における黒湯の地球化学的特徴 |journal=日本地球化学会年会要旨集 |volume=66 |issue= |publisher=一般社団法人日本地球化学会 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>。
== 溶存有機物以外の黒湯 == 「黒湯」と称される温泉には、植物が微生物によって分解された腐植質を含む地層に由来するものとは別に、硫化水素型の硫黄泉などで[[硫化水素]]が[[鉄]]が化合して黒色の硫化鉄を析出して懸濁しているものなどもある<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=第13話 様々な温泉の色 |publisher=岐阜県温泉協会 |date= |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>。硫化鉄の析出による黒湯がみられる例として[[塩原元湯温泉]]があり「墨湯」ともいう<ref name="gifu" />。 * [[塩原元湯温泉]]<ref name="gifu" /> * [[岩室温泉]]<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=区役所だより にしかん 2023年8月号 |publisher=新潟市西蒲区 |date= |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <!-- === 注釈 === <references group="注"/> === 出典 === --> {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[にごり湯]]
{{温泉}} {{デフォルトソート:くろゆ}} [[Category:温泉]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黒湯]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11678460]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{otheruses|[[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Onsen|qq=}}, a hot spring in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita Prefecture]]}}
'''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.

== Overview ==
Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor hot springs|qq=}}) in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], while in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kanto region]], they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower [[:en:Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio|C/N ratio]] and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/C/P ratio|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135506362}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.

Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|qq=}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.

== Non-Humic Kuroyu ==
Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:

* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|qq=}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" />
* {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamuro Onsen|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

== References ==

{{Reflist}}

== Related Articles ==

Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot springs|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor hot springs|qq=}}) in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], while in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kanto region]], they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|qq=}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.

== Non-Humic Kuroyu ==
Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:

* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|qq=}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" />
* {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamuro Onsen|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黒湯]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11678460]]
Wikidata name: (none)
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{otheruses|[[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Onsen|qq=}}, a hot spring in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita Prefecture]]}}
'''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.

== Overview ==
Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor hot springs|qq=}}) in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], while in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kanto region]], they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower [[:en:Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio|C/N ratio]] and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/C/P ratio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135506362}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.

Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|qq=}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.

== Non-Humic Kuroyu ==
Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:

* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|qq=}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" />
* {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamuro Onsen|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

== References ==

[[ja:黒湯]]
<!--Kuroyu--><!--Kuroyu--><!--Kuroyu--> [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:Hot Spring Types]] {{wikidata link|Q11678460}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黒湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11678460]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Onsen|qq=}}, a hot spring in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita Prefecture]]}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor hot springs|qq=}}) in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], while in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kanto region]], they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower [[:en:Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio|C/N ratio]] and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/C/P ratio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135506362}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|qq=}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|qq=}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamuro Onsen|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>
== References ==
{{Reflist}}

Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:

Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|qq=}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamuro Onsen|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黒湯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黒湯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黒湯]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11678460]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{otheruses|[[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Onsen|qq=}}, a hot spring in [[:en:Semboku, Akita|Semboku, Akita Prefecture]]}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor hot springs|qq=}}) in [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]], while in the [[:en:Kantō region|Kanto region]], they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower [[:en:Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio|C/N ratio]] and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/C/P ratio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135506362}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|qq=}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|qq=}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamuro Onsen|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|qq=}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|qq=}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamuro Onsen|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor hot springs|qq=}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|WD=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido}}, while in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kanto region}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio|en|Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio|WD=Q665261|lt=C/N ratio}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/C/P ratio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135506362}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|qq=}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|qq=}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamuro Onsen|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio|en|Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio|WD=Q665261|lt=C/N ratio}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/C/P ratio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135506362}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|qq=}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|qq=}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamuro Onsen|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

{{wikidata link|Q11678460}} [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hot Spring Types]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] [[Category:Kuroyu]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黒湯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黒湯]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:黒湯]]
{{otheruses|{{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen|1=Onsen}} type|{{ill|Kuroyu Onsen|ja|黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Onsen|qq=}}, a hot spring in {{ill|Semboku, Akita|en|Semboku, Akita|WD=Q846877|lt=Semboku, Akita Prefecture|1=Semboku, Akita}}}} '''Kuroyu''' (黒湯) refers to hot springs containing transparent, brown-colored humic substances such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="inoue" />. Additionally, some hot springs are called "Kuroyu" or "Sumiyu" due to their black coloration caused by other factors, such as iron sulfide<ref name="gifu" />.
== Overview == Kuroyu refers to brown-colored hot springs, also known as "brown hot springs," due to their coloring by dissolved organic matter (DOM), such as humic acid and fulvic acid<ref name="takano2018">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Uchino Eiji |author3= Aoyagi Naoki |title=Carbon, Nitrogen, and Phosphorus Contents in Dissolved Organic Substances and Humic Substances in Brown Hot Springs in Hokkaido |journal=Japanese Journal of Limnology |volume=79 |issue=3 |publisher=Japanese Society of Limnology |year=2018-09 |pages=169-178 |url=https://agriknowledge.affrc.go.jp/RN/2010925649.pdf}}</ref>. Depending on the region, such springs are called "Moor-type hot springs" ({{ill|Moor springs|ja|モール泉|1=Moor hot springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor springs|13=User:Immanuelle/Moor hot springs|qq=}}) in {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|WD=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido|1=Hokkaido}}, while in the {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kanto region|1=Kantō region}}, they are often referred to as "Kuroyu"<ref name="takano2018" />. There are also compositional differences; for example, Kuroyu in Yokohama and Kawasaki in Kanagawa Prefecture shows lower {{ill|Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio|en|Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio|WD=Q665261|lt=C/N ratio|1=Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio}} and higher {{ill|C/P ratio|ja|C/P比|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/C/P ratio|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135506362|1=Carbon-to-phosphorus ratio}} compared to Moor-type hot springs in Hokkaido<ref>{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Takano Keishi |author2=Inoue Genki |author3= Uchino Eiji |title=Characteristics of Humic Substances in Kuroyu Springs in Yokohama and Kawasaki Based on Carbon/Nitrogen and Carbon/Phosphorus Ratios |journal=Onsen Science |volume=70 |issue=3 |year=2020 |pages=137-149 |url=http://www.j-hss.org/journal/back_number/vol70_pdf/vol70no3_137_149.pdf}}</ref>.
Although there are no officially defined therapeutic effects specifically for brown-colored hot springs, they are often promoted as being beneficial for smoothing the skin<ref name="takano2018" />. Notable examples of Kuroyu in the Kanto region include the {{ill|Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|ja|大田区の黒湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuroyu Hot Spring in Ota Ward, Tokyo|qq=}}<ref name="inoue">{{Cite journal |和書 |author1=Inoue Genki |author2=Shimizu Risako |author3= Nagura Mika |title=Geochemical Characteristics of Kuroyu in Ota Ward, Tokyo |journal=Abstracts of the Annual Meeting of the Geochemical Society of Japan |volume=66 |year=2019 |pages=124 |url=https://doi.org/10.14862/geochemproc.66.0_124}}</ref>.
== Non-Humic Kuroyu == Some hot springs called "Kuroyu" do not originate from humic substances decomposed from plants but rather result from other processes, such as the precipitation of iron sulfide. This type includes sulfur springs where hydrogen sulfide reacts with iron to form black iron sulfide in suspension<ref name="gifu">{{Cite web |url=http://www.gifu-onsen.jp/topic_museum/no_13.html |title=Episode 13: Various Colors of Hot Springs |publisher=Gifu Prefecture Hot Spring Association |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>. Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
* {{ill|Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|ja|塩原元湯温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shiobara Motoyu Onsen|qq=}} – also called "Sumiyu"<ref name="gifu" /> * {{ill|Iwamuro Onsen|ja|岩室温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwamuro Onsen|qq=}}<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.niigata.lg.jp/nishikan/kohoshi/kouhou/r05/nishikan_0806/pdf/392_3.pdf |title=Nishikan Ward Newsletter, August 2023 |publisher=Niigata City Nishikan Ward Office |accessdate=2024-03-14}}</ref>

Examples of iron sulfide-based Kuroyu include:
